protocol_definition.name: Sequential Handoff
protocol_definition.visibility: Each agent sees the task and upstream outputs only; no backward messages.
protocol_definition.termination: Stop after one forward pass and Writer output.

intermediate_messages.content:
Execution plan
- Collect current official product information for Slack and Microsoft Teams from their websites.
- Extract five aspects for each product: target users, core communication features, collaboration/integrations, main strength, main limitation.
- Build a side-by-side comparison table with the required columns.
- Write a concise market overview (1–2 short paragraphs).
- Provide a recommendation for a small remote company with daily messaging, file sharing, video meetings, and basic tool integrations, with evidence-based justification.
- List sources with direct evidence citations.

Deliverables

1. Brief market overview
2. Product comparison table
3. Recommendation paragraph
4. Source list

Product comparison (based on current official sites)

| Product | Target Users | Core Communication Features | Collaboration and Integrations | Main Strength | Main Limitation |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Slack | Small teams and organizations seeking focused, channel-based collaboration; widely adopted by remote and distributed teams | Real-time messaging (channels, direct messages), voice/video calls, screen sharing, reminders, threads | App integrations gallery, workflows, file sharing via Slack, searchable historical messages, Slack Connect for external collaboration | Highly extensible with a large app ecosystem and extensible automation (workflows) | May require multiple apps/workarounds for some enterprise governance and advanced compliance; can become noisy with many channels |
| Microsoft Teams | Organizations of all sizes with Microsoft 365 alignment; strong integration for Office apps | Persistent chat, video meetings, calls, background blur, meeting recordings, chat threads | Deep integration with SharePoint/OneDrive, Office apps (Word/Excel/PowerPoint), Planner, To Do, Power Platform; file collaboration in Office apps | Tight integration with Microsoft 365, seamless co-authoring and file storage in OneDrive/SharePoint | Can feel heavy/complex for small teams; reliance on Microsoft ecosystem may be limiting if not using full Microsoft 365 |

Recommendation
For a small remote company requiring daily messaging, file sharing, video meetings, and basic integration with other workplace tools, Slack is recommended if the priority is a lightweight, flexible chat-first experience with a broad app ecosystem readily enabling quick integrations beyond a Microsoft-centric stack. Slack’s strong emphasis on real-time messaging, searchable history, and a wide range of third-party integrations makes it easier to connect diverse tools without committing to a single suite. If the company already uses Microsoft 365 extensively and values seamless Office document co-authoring and governance within a familiar environment, Teams offers a compelling alternative, with deep Office integration and collaboration around SharePoint/OneDrive. However, for a small remote team not tied to Microsoft-heavy workflows, Slack provides quicker setup and a broader integration surface to support daily messaging and file sharing with video meetings.

Sources
- Slack official product pages (targets, features, integrations, strengths/limitations)
- Microsoft Teams official product pages (targets, features, integrations, strengths/limitations)

1. Brief market overview
Both Slack and Microsoft Teams are leading collaboration platforms aimed at enabling remote or distributed teams to communicate, share files, and coordinate work. Slack positions itself as a flexible, channel-based messaging hub with a broad ecosystem of third-party apps and customizable workflows. Teams emphasizes deep integration with Microsoft 365 (Office apps, SharePoint, OneDrive) and persistent chat, meetings, and co-authoring within a single productivity suite. For a small remote company, the choice often hinges on preference for a chat-first, app-centric workflow (Slack) versus a tightly integrated Microsoft 365 environment (Teams).

2. Product comparison table

| Product | Target Users | Core Communication Features | Collaboration and Integrations | Main Strength | Main Limitation |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Slack | Small teams and organizations seeking focused, channel-based collaboration; widely adopted by remote and distributed teams | Real-time messaging (channels, direct messages), voice/video calls, screen sharing, reminders, threads | App integrations gallery, workflows, file sharing via Slack, searchable historical messages, Slack Connect for external collaboration | Highly extensible with a large app ecosystem and extensible automation (workflows) | May require multiple apps/workarounds for some enterprise governance and advanced compliance; can become noisy with many channels |
| Microsoft Teams | Organizations of all sizes with Microsoft 365 alignment; strong integration for Office apps | Persistent chat, video meetings, calls, background blur, meeting recordings, chat threads | Deep integration with SharePoint/OneDrive, Office apps (Word/Excel/PowerPoint), Planner, To Do, Power Platform; file collaboration in Office apps | Tight integration with Microsoft 365, seamless co-authoring and file storage in OneDrive/SharePoint | Can feel heavy/complex for small teams; reliance on Microsoft ecosystem may be limiting if not using full Microsoft 365 |

3. Recommendation paragraph
For a small remote company that needs daily messaging, file sharing, video meetings, and basic integration with other workplace tools, Slack is recommended if the priority is a lightweight, flexible chat-first experience with broad app integrations. Slack’s real-time messaging, searchable history, and large ecosystem of third-party apps and workflows facilitate quick connections to a variety of tools without committing to a single vendor stack. If the company already relies heavily on Microsoft 365 and values seamless Office document co-authoring and governance within a familiar environment, Microsoft Teams offers a compelling alternative with deep Office integration and co-authoring capabilities. However, for a small remote team not bound to Microsoft-centric workflows, Slack typically provides quicker setup, an extensive integration surface, and straightforward daily messaging and file-sharing with video meetings.

4. Source list
- Slack official product pages (target users, core features, integrations, strengths/limitations): Slack Channels, Slack Features, Slack Apps & Integrations, Slack Connect, Slack Workflows
- Microsoft Teams official product pages (target users, core features, integrations, strengths/limitations): Microsoft Teams Features, Office 365 integration with Teams (SharePoint/OneDrive, Word/Excel/PowerPoint), Teams Meet for video meetings, Power Platform integrations

1. Brief market overview
Slack and Microsoft Teams are leading collaboration platforms for remote teams, each with a distinct emphasis. Slack positions itself as a lightweight, channel-based messaging hub with a broad ecosystem of third-party apps and customizable workflows. Teams centers on deep Microsoft 365 integration, offering persistent chat, meetings, co-authoring, and file storage within SharePoint/OneDrive. For a small remote company, the choice often hinges on preferring a chat-first, app-rich workflow (Slack) versus a tightly integrated Microsoft 365 environment (Teams).

2. Product comparison table

| Product | Target Users | Core Communication Features | Collaboration and Integrations | Main Strength | Main Limitation |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Slack | Small teams and organizations seeking focused, channel-based collaboration; widely adopted by remote and distributed teams | Real-time messaging (channels, direct messages), voice/video calls, screen sharing, reminders, threads | App integrations gallery, workflows, file sharing via Slack, searchable historical messages, Slack Connect for external collaboration | Highly extensible with a large app ecosystem and extensible automation (workflows) | May require multiple apps/workarounds for some enterprise governance and advanced compliance; can become noisy with many channels |
| Microsoft Teams | Organizations of all sizes with Microsoft 365 alignment; strong integration for Office apps | Persistent chat, video meetings, calls, background blur, meeting recordings, chat threads | Deep integration with SharePoint/OneDrive, Office apps (Word/Excel/PowerPoint), Planner, To Do, Power Platform; file collaboration in Office apps | Tight integration with Microsoft 365, seamless co-authoring and file storage in OneDrive/SharePoint | Can feel heavy/complex for small teams; reliance on Microsoft ecosystem may be limiting if not using full Microsoft 365 |

3. Recommendation paragraph
For a small remote company that needs daily messaging, file sharing, video meetings, and basic integration with other workplace tools, Slack is recommended if the priority is a lightweight, flexible chat-first experience with broad app integrations. Slack’s real-time messaging, searchable history, and extensive third-party integrations support quick connections to diverse tools without committing to a single vendor stack. If the company already relies heavily on Microsoft 365 and values seamless Office document co-authoring and governance, Teams offers a compelling alternative with deep Office integration and co-authoring capabilities. However, for a small remote team not tied to Microsoft-centric workflows, Slack typically provides quicker setup, a broader integration surface, and straightforward daily messaging and file-sharing with video meetings.

4. Source list
- Slack official product pages (target users, core features, integrations, strengths/limitations): Slack Channels, Slack Features, Slack Apps & Integrations, Slack Connect, Slack Workflows
- Microsoft Teams official product pages (target users, core features, integrations, strengths/limitations): Microsoft Teams Features, Office 365 integration with Teams (SharePoint/OneDrive, Word/Excel/PowerPoint), Teams Meet for video meetings, Power Platform integrations

3. Collaboration and integration features

2. Product comparison table

4. Source list

Slack positions itself as a flexible, channel-based messaging hub with a broad ecosystem of third-party apps and customizable workflows. Teams centers on deep Microsoft 365 integration, offering persistent chat, meetings, co-authoring, and file storage within SharePoint/OneDrive. 3. Recommendation paragraph
For a small remote company that needs daily messaging, file sharing, video meetings, and basic integration with other workplace tools, Slack is recommended if the priority is a lightweight, flexible chat-first experience with broad app integrations. Slack’s real-time messaging, searchable history, and extensive third-party integrations support quick connections to diverse tools without committing to a single vendor stack. If the company already relies heavily on Microsoft 365 and values seamless Office document co-authoring and governance, Teams offers a compelling alternative with deep Office integration and co-authoring capabilities. However, for a small remote team not tied to Microsoft-centric workflows, Slack typically provides quicker setup, a broader integration surface, and straightforward daily messaging and file-sharing with video meetings.

4. Source list
- Slack official product pages (target users, core features, integrations, strengths/limitations)
- Microsoft Teams official product pages (target users, core features, integrations, strengths/limitations)
